title: Pseudospigelia polystachya
taxonomy: Pseudospigelia polystachya is classified within the family Apocynaceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants commonly known as the dogbane or oleander family. It is further classified as a genus within the tribe Plumerieae. Within the Apocynaceae family, Pseudospigelia polystachya is a member of the subfamily Apocynoideae. This plant is native to Asia and its classification reflects its unique characteristics and evolutionary history within the Apocynaceae family. The classification of Pseudospigelia polystachya provides valuable insights into its relationships with other plant species.
uses: Pseudospigelia polystachya, also known as the False Snakewort, has been traditionally used in folk medicine for various purposes. The plant's roots, leaves, and stems are said to possess anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and antimicrobial properties. Traditionally, the plant is used to treat skin conditions, such as wounds, eczema, and dermatitis. Its roots are also believed to have astringent properties, which may help in treating diarrhea and other gastrointestinal issues. However, more research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of using Pseudospigelia polystachya for medicinal purposes.

intro: Pseudospigelia marilandica is a plant species commonly referred to as Pseudospigelia polystachya, however the most widely used name in the botanical community is Pseudospigelia marilandica, but its most widely used name is Pseudospigelia marilandica or Pseudospigelia marilandica 'marilandica',  however, in herbal medicine, this plant is often referred to as Indian Cough Root or Spigelia. The health benefits of Pseudospigelia marilandica include its use in treating respiratory issues such as coughs and bronchitis, as well as its anti-inflammatory properties. The bioactive constituents of Pseudospigelia marilandica include alkaloids such as spigeline, spigelinine, and spigemine, which contribute to its therapeutic effects. In herbal medicine, Pseudospigelia marilandica is often made into tinctures, decoctions, or infusions, with the most common preparations being cough syrups and expectorant teas.
